--- SOURCE 1: Introducing digital risk twin for disaster risk management - Nature ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x

SUMMARY:
The digital risk twin (DRT) adapts digital twin (DT) for disaster risk management (DRM) by integrating automated and manual data collection methods, such as IoT, remote sensing, surveys and field observations, while incorporating human-in-the-loop decision-making for flexible and effective interventions, maintaining real-time virtual simulations, and addressing disaster scenario challenges. DRTs can adjust to the evolving nature of a disaster or risk scenario (e.g., during an earthquake, as new damage reports and access conditions emerge, the DRT continuously updates response priorities and resource deployment), where conditions change rapidly, and real-time automated intervention is not always feasible or effective. This scenario demonstrates how the DRT can support disaster response by integrating hybrid data sources, enabling human-in-the-loop decision-making, and maintaining real-time adaptability, even when digital infrastructure is partially degraded or not fully available.

Rethinking digital twin: Introducing digital risk twin for disaster risk management
===================================================================================

*   [Saman Ghaffarian](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#auth-Saman-Ghaffarian-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#Aff1)

[_npj Natural Hazards_](https://www.nature.com/npjnathazards)**volume 2**, Article number:79 (2025) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#citeas)

Abstract
--------

The digital risk twin (DRT) adapts digital twin (DT) for disaster risk management (DRM) by integrating automated and manual data collection methods, such as IoT, remote sensing, surveys and field observations, while incorporating human-in-the-loop decision-making for flexible and effective interventions, maintaining real-time virtual simulations, and addressing disaster scenario challenges.

Natural hazard-induced disasters are escalating in frequency and intensity due to climate change, urbanisation, and environmental degradation. These events pose significant challenges to global resilience and demand advanced tools to predict, prepare for, and respond to such crises effectively. Digital Twins (DTs) have proven effective in various industries by providing real-time simulations and optimisations[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR1 "Grieves, M. & Vickers, J. in Transdisciplinary Perspectives on Complex Systems Ch. Chapter 4, 85–113 (2017)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR2 "Rosen, R., von Wichert, G., Lo, G. & Bettenhausen, K. D. About the importance of autonomy and digital twins for the future of manufacturing. IFAC-PapersOnLine 48, 567–572 (2015)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR3 "Mendi, A. F., Erol, T. & Doğan, D. Digital twin in the military field. IEEE Internet Comput. 26, 33–40 (2022)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR4 "Verdouw, C., Tekinerdogan, B., Beulens, A. & Wolfert, S. Digital twins in smart farming. Agric. Syst. 189, 103046 (2021)."). However, their application in Disaster Risk Management (DRM) is limited due to their reliance on automated data collection and direct implementation of insights, which may not always be feasible in disaster scenarios[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR5 "Lagap, U. & Ghaffarian, S. Digital post-disaster risk management twinning: A review and improved conceptual framework. Int. J. Disaster Risk Reduct. 110, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2024.104629
                  
                 (2024)."). Recent studies have begun addressing these limitations by proposing hybrid digital twin models that incorporate both manual and automated data inputs[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR6 "Braik, A. M. & Koliou, M. A digital twin framework for efficient electric power restoration and resilient recovery in the aftermath of hurricanes considering the interdependencies with road network and essential facilities. Resilient Cities Struct. 3, 79–91 (2024)."),[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR7 "Zhang, C., Fan, C., Yao, W., Hu, X. & Mostafavi, A. Social media for intelligent public information and warning in disasters: An interdisciplinary review. Int. J. Inf. Manag. 49, 190–207 (2019)."), as well as frameworks that integrate human-in-the-loop decision-making in disaster contexts[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR8 "Alibrandi, U. Risk-Informed Digital Twin of Buildings and Infrastructures for Sustainable and Resilient Urban Communities. ASCE-ASME J. Risk Uncertain. Eng. Syst., Part A: Civ. Eng. 8, 04022032 (2022)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR9 "Fan, C., Zhang, C., Yahja, A. & Mostafavi, A. Disaster City Digital Twin: A vision for integrating artificial and human intelligence for disaster management. Int. J. Inf. Manag. 56, 102049 (2021)."). However, these contributions tend to focus on specific, isolated applications—such as infrastructure resilience or post-disaster logistics—rather than offering a unified, cross-sectoral framework. Building on these foundational efforts, we introduce a more integrated and holistic concept—the Digital Risk Twin (DRT)—which addresses the complex, multi-disciplinary and interconnected dimensions of DRM, including physical, environmental, social, economic, and institutional factors. DRT is grounded in the key structural components of DTs, enabling both automated and manual data collection methods, such as remote sensing, social sensing, surveys and field observations, and emphasises a human-in-the-loop decision-making process where insights are used by governments and decision-makers for automated and manual implementation, rather than only immediate automated action. While doing so, it maintains a continuous flow of real-time monitoring data, allowing users to quickly begin observing the outcomes and take further action as needed. This flexibility makes DRTs especially valuable in DRM, where complex decision-making and local context play crucial roles. The components, advantages, and applications of DRTs are explored, highlighting their potential to bridge the gap between Digital Twins and Digital Shadows (DSs)[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR10 "Sepasgozar, S. M. E. Differentiating digital twin from digital shadow: elucidating a paradigm shift to expedite a smart, sustainable built environment. Buildings 11, 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/buildings11040151
                  
                 (2021)."),[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR11 "Errandonea, I., Beltrán, S. & Arrizabalaga, S. Digital Twin for maintenance: a literature review. Computers Ind. 123, 103316 (2020)."). The introduction of DRTs offers a dynamic and scalable approach to disaster preparedness, early warning, response, and recovery, ultimately enhancing resilience in the face of such events.

Digital Twins (DTs), Digital Shadows (DSs) and Their Components
---------------------------------------------------------------

DTs, originally developed as a concept by NASA[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR2 "Rosen, R., von Wichert, G., Lo, G. & Bettenhausen, K. D. About the importance of autonomy and digital twins for the future of manufacturing. IFAC-PapersOnLine 48, 567–572 (2015)."), are virtual replicas of physical entities or systems that enable bidirectional interaction by integrating real-time data to simulate, predict, and optimise performance[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR12 "Barricelli, B. R., Casiraghi, E. & Fogli, D. A survey on digital twin: definitions, characteristics, applications, and design implications. IEEE Access 7, 167653–167671 (2019)."). In contrast, DSs are simplified virtual models with unidirectional data flow designed for passive observation and analysis without automated feedback[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR13 "Becker, F. et al. in Conceptual Modeling. (eds Ghose, A. et al.) 271–281 (Springer International Publishing)."),[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR14 "Brecher, C., Dalibor, M., Rumpe, B., Schilling, K. & Wortmann, A. An ecosystem for digital shadows in manufacturing. Procedia CIRP 104, 833–838 (2021)."). Typical DT and DS systems comprise three main components:

1.   1.
Physical Entity: The real-world object or system being modelled.

2.   2.
Digital Replica: A virtual representation that mirrors the physical entity’s state.

3.   3.
Data Linkage: Continuous, real-time synchronisation between the physical entity and its digital counterpart via sensors and IoT devices. Bidirectional interaction with automated intervention characterises DTs, whereas DSs involve passive monitoring and modelling with unidirectional data flow.

Introducing Digital Risk Twins
------------------------------

Digital Risk Twins (DRTs) offer a nuanced evolution of DTs, designed specifically for environments where automated data collection and immediate, automated interventions are not feasible. While DRTs share core characteristics with DTs—such as the ability to represent physical systems in a virtual space and to dynamically simulate, predict, and optimise system behaviour—they also acknowledge the realities addressed by DSs, where data often flows unidirectionally from the physical system to a digital model, supporting analysis and decision-making without automated feedback loops[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR15 "Riaz, K., McAfee, M. & Gharbia, S. S. Management of climate resilience: exploring the potential of digital twin technology, 3D city modelling, and early warning systems. Sensors 23, 2659 (2023)."),[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR16 "Wright, L. & Davidson, S. How to tell the difference between a model and a digital twin. Adv. Modeling Simul. Eng. Sci. 7, 13 (2020).").

In DT systems, data is typically collected automatically through IoT sensors and other digital means, ensuring continuous, real-time updates. Insights generated by the digital replica are often fed back directly into the physical system for real-time optimisation or intervention. In contrast, DRTs allow for manual data collection alongside automatic methods, reflecting the realities of complex environments such as DRM, where data may be gathered through traditional methods such as surveys, human observation, and situational reporting. This flexibility in data collection methods is especially valuable in DRM, where sensors may not be ubiquitous, and human-driven data sources are critical for assessing certain aspects of a disaster or risk scenario (e.g., social, environmental, or local economic conditions)[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR5 "Lagap, U. & Ghaffarian, S. Digital post-disaster risk management twinning: A review and improved conceptual framework. Int. J. Disaster Risk Reduct. 110, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2024.104629
                  
                 (2024)."),[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR17 "Ariyachandra, M. R. M. F. & Wedawatta, G. Digital Twin Smart Cities for Disaster Risk Management: A Review of Evolving Concepts. Sustainability 15, 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su151511910
                  
                 (2023)."),[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR18 "Cui, Y. et al. A digital-twin platform for cryospheric disaster warning. Natl. Sci. Rev. 11, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1093/nsr/nwae300
                  
                 (2024).").

Similarly, the insights from DTs typically lead to immediate automated actions, whereas DRTs introduce a human-in-the-loop decision-making process. The insights derived from the DRT—whether from automated or manually collected data—do not need to be directly implemented via automated systems. Instead, these insights can be shared with governments, emergency response teams, and other decision-makers, who can then process the information, conduct further analysis, and make informed decisions. These decisions, in turn, may require manual implementation, such as coordinating evacuation plans, dispatching resources, or deploying rescue teams. At the same time, the system continues to deliver updated situational data, ensuring decision-makers have ongoing feedback to support timely adjustments and informed follow-up actions. This model is particularly suited for DRM, where human expertise, judgement, and local context play a critical role in the response and recovery phases. DRTs share certain process characteristics with Urban Digital Twins[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR9 "Fan, C., Zhang, C., Yahja, A. & Mostafavi, A. Disaster City Digital Twin: A vision for integrating artificial and human intelligence for disaster management. Int. J. Inf. Manag. 56, 102049 (2021)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR19 "Huang, H. & Tsou, J. Y. in Advances in Information Technology in Civil and Building Engineering. (eds Francis, A., Miresco, E. & Melhado, S.) 536–557 (Springer Nature Switzerland)."), such as partial system representation and manual decision-making, but differ by integrating social, economic, environmental, and institutional dimensions as core components and by extending beyond urban applications[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#ref-CR20 "Mazzetto, S. A Review of Urban Digital Twins Integration, Challenges, and Future Directions in Smart City Development. Sustainability 16, 8337 (2024).").

Thus, DRTs provide real-time data-driven insights but prioritise a collaborative, multi-step implementation process over immediate automation. This flexibility in handling data and applying insights makes DRTs particularly effective for complex, dynamic systems such as DRM, where diverse stakeholders interact, situations evolve constantly, and the inherent dynamism cannot be fully captured by IoT networks or automated platforms.

**_Key Features of Digital Risk Twins_****(**Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44304-025-00135-x#Fig1)**)**

1.   1.
Flexible and Scalable Data Collection: DRTs enable hybrid data acquisition by integrating both automated sources (e.g., IoT sensors) and manual inputs (e.g., surveys, field observations), allowing more comprehensive coverage. This approach supports multi-disciplinary applications and scalability by accommodating diverse real-time, historical, digital, and manual data streams from multiple stakeholders and sectors, essential for large-scale disaster scenarios.

2.   2.
Human-Driven Decision-Making: Insights generated by DRTs can be processed by decision-makers, such as government agencies, local authorities, and emergency response teams, who then decide on the best course of action. This allows for contextual, scenario-based decision-making in environments where human input is crucial.

3.   3.
Real-Time, Context-Sensitive Adaptation: DTs are generally designed for real-time, closed-loop interactions, but DRTs focus on providing adaptable, context-sensitive data integration. DRTs can adjust to the evolving nature of a disaster or risk scenario (e.g., during an earthquake, as new damage reports and access conditions emerge, the DRT continuously updates response priorities and resource deployment), where conditions change rapidly, and real-time automated intervention is not always feasible or effective.

Why Digital Risk Twins are Ideal for DRM
----------------------------------------

DRT represents a middle ground between DT and DS, providing the flexibility to handle both automatic and manual processes, and the adaptability to work within the complex and dynamic nature of DRM. Their ability to process a combination of data sources (automated, manual, or a mix) and integrate human decision-making into the process makes them uniquely suited for real-world DRM applications.

In DRM, the decisions made during a disaster response—such as evacuation routes, resource allocation, or emergency interventions—often require human judgement, local knowledge, and coordination among various stakeholders. While real-time simulation and predictive capabilities can be useful, the human element of interpretation and implementation is indispensable. DRTs leverage contextual insights and integrate dynamic hybrid data streams to enable adaptive, real-time decision-making in complex disaster scenarios. Unlike conventional decision-support systems built on rigid data workflows, DRTs are designed to adjust to rapidly changing conditions, supporting human-in-the-loop responses that empower governments and organisations to make informed decisions, swiftly begin observing their outcomes, and further act upon them.

Applications of Digital Risk Twins in DRM
-----------------------------------------

DRTs offer numerous applications across the disaster management phases:

1.   1.
**Preparedness:** (Near) real-time risk assessment and scenario modelling improve planning and resource prepositioning.

2.   2.
**Early Warning:** DRTs enhance early warning systems by integrating diverse datasets for accurate predictions and delivering actionable insights to responders and communities.

3.   3.
**Response:** Dynamic monitoring and optimisation of emergency response operations, such as resource allocation, route optimisation, and incident prioritisation, enable more effective interventions.

4.   4.
**Recovery:** Post-disaster analysis to identify lessons learned, monitor and evaluate recovery strategies, and enhance long-term resilience.

5.   5.
**Resilience Building:** DRT-based continuous risk modelling, vulnerability assessment, and resilience evaluation guide adaptive and transformative actions, enabling systems to evolve incrementally or fundamentally in response to changing risks and vulnerabilities.

Early warning scenarios, where human decision-making plays a critical role, provide a notable example. Human behavioural modelling is a crucial component of DRM, offering valuable insights into evacuation patterns and disaster response dynamics. Agent-based modelling (ABM) is seen as a highly suitable core simulation model for DTs. However, its integration has faced challenges, such as incorporating human behaviour based on non-automated data collection, highlighting a new concept and framework for such systems. With DRT, ABM can now be fundamentally and conceptually integrated more effectively, enabling diverse applications across DRM scenarios. This integration provides deeper insights into individual and group behaviours under varying conditions, highlighting the transformative potential of DRTs in managing complex and evolving disaster scenarios.

A comprehensive illustrative case study demonstrating the application of the DRT concept in a multi-hazard scenario is provided in Appendix A, further clarifying its scope and capabilities.

Challenges and Future Considerations
------------------------------------

The implementation of DRTs is not without challenges. Key considerations include:

*   **Technical Barriers:** High computational demands and integration complexity can hinder the seamless deployment of DRTs in disaster scenarios.

*   **Ethical Concerns:** Privacy and data security issues associated with (near) real-time data streams need robust measures to protect sensitive information.

*   **Resource Requirements:** Significant investments in infrastructure, training, and skilled personnel are essential to fully leverage DRT capabilities.

*   **Data Collection Limitations:** Automated data collection using IoT devices and sensors provides real-time inputs, but certain socio-cultural aspects and community-specific insights require manual data collection methods, such as surveys or interviews. These are particularly crucial for understanding human behaviour, societal dynamics, and localised disaster impacts.

*   **Real-Time Data Challenges:** Real-time data collection is not always feasible, especially in chaotic and disaster situations, where data availability and reliability can be significantly challenged; therefore, historical and contextually rich data can play a more prominent role. Furthermore, the meaning of “real-time” can vary depending on the context. Real-time modelling is highly effective during the early warning and response phases, but it may be less crucial in recovery.

Addressing these challenges will require interdisciplinary collaboration and supportive policy frameworks. For instance, international standards on data sharing and security could help mitigate ethical concerns, and government funding along with private sector partnerships could reduce the barriers to DRT adoption. Additionally, balancing automated and manual data collection methods and tailoring the use of real-time and historical data according to the DRM phase will enhance the effectiveness of DRTs.

Appendix A. Digital Risk Twinning for Earthquake-Based Multi-Hazard Response
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

To illustrate the practical relevance and necessity of the Digital Risk Twin (DRT) concept, this appendix presents a hypothetical yet plausible scenario inspired by real-world events such as the 2023 Kahramanmaraş earthquakes in Türkiye. These events resulted in widespread structural collapse, cascading infrastructure failures, and complex humanitarian challenges—conditions under which conventional digital systems are often rendered ineffective. This scenario demonstrates how the DRT can support disaster response by integrating hybrid data sources, enabling human-in-the-loop decision-making, and maintaining real-time adaptability, even when digital infrastructure is partially degraded or not fully available. The sections below outline the situation and key components of the DRT, highlighting its significance and adaptability to complex, multi-sectoral disaster response scenarios within this specific context.

1. Physical Twin: The Real-World Disaster Environment
-----------------------------------------------------

A high-magnitude earthquake strikes a mountainous urban region, triggering a series of interconnected hazards. Structural collapses occur across residential and public buildings, while landslides block critical transportation corridors. The initial seismic event, combined with secondary hazards, leads to cascading failures in power, water, and communication infrastructure, further complicating emergency response efforts. Tens of thousands of people are displaced, many from vulnerable populations, and access to reliable situational data becomes increasingly constrained. This scenario reflects the complexity and interdependence typical of multi-hazard, cascading disasters, where the physical system becomes fragmented and difficult to monitor through sensor-based digital technologies alone.

2. Data Collection and Processing
---------------------------------

In the immediate aftermath, ground-based sensors and communication infrastructure are significantly compromised due to power outages, physical damage to IoT devices, and network disruptions. Under these conditions, the DRT integrates diverse data sources, including historical data, to establish a usable and realistic situational overview.

Manual data collection becomes essential. Field observations by engineers and first responders are recorded and reported to assess structural damage, road accessibility, and ongoing hazards. Community-level data is gathered via offline-capable mobile applications, allowing residents to report injuries, shelter needs, and missing persons. Simultaneously, local volunteers and NGOs contribute structured situational reports through coordinated community networks. Social media and SMS-based inputs, where communication networks are still available, provide additional behavioural and logistical information, particularly in areas not covered by formal channels.

In parallel, remote sensing technologies—such as UAVs and satellite imagery—are deployed to capture high-resolution data, with a specific focus on inaccessible areas. Advanced processing techniques, including AI-based image recognition and machine learning algorithms, are applied to these remote sensing datasets to extract actionable insights. For example, damaged structures, blocked roads, or emerging landslide zones can be identified and mapped automatically, significantly reducing the time between data acquisition and information/knowledge extraction. All data, whether collected manually or remotely, undergo cleaning, geo-tagging, and formatting to ensure consistency before integration into the digital twin environment.

3. Within the Digital Twin: Modelling and Scenario Simulation
-------------------------------------------------------------

The DRT assimilates processed data into a dynamic virtual model that reflects the evolving disaster context in real time. Within this environment, multiple modelling approaches can be applied to support operational decision-making.

For example, Agent-based modelling (ABM) is used to simulate the behaviours and interactions of individuals, communities, and institutions. These simulations help to anticipate evacuation flows, estimate shelter demand, and identify coordination bottlenecks across sectors and jurisdictions. The ability to test and compare alternative strategies—such as rerouting emergency supplies or prioritising certain geographic areas for rescue—is critical to improving outcomes under high uncertainty.

## Digital twins for disaster prevention

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/668e215d8bbef917ac3b8892/6718995cfb9b4daddd014ba4_landvault19.webp)

**Digital twins** are advanced virtual models that mirror physical objects, systems, or processes, enabling real-time simulation, analysis, and control. By integrating data from sensors, historical records, and predictive analytics, digital twins provide a dynamic, data-driven representation of their real-world counterparts, allowing for enhanced monitoring, diagnostics, and optimization of performance and behavior. For instance, GE creates digital twins that enable predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and optimization of complex systems by creating virtual replicas of physical infrastructure.

Digital twins are invaluable in disaster prevention, predicting, preparing for, and mitigating the impacts of natural and man-made disasters. They allow for early warning and risk assessment by simulating various disaster scenarios, helping authorities deploy resources efficiently, and enhancing infrastructure resilience. Additionally, **digital twins** facilitate effective recovery and reconstruction by providing detailed damage assessments and planning insights, ultimately making communities more resilient to future crises.

### Understanding digital twins in the context of disaster prevention

Digital twins can forecast potential disaster impacts by simulating various scenarios, such as hurricanes, earthquakes, or industrial accidents. These 3D virtual replicas can also track weather patterns to create a comprehensive view of the likely impact of impending disasters. This capability allows authorities to anticipate the effects on infrastructure, plan effective responses, and implement mitigation strategies, ultimately reducing the risk of damage and enhancing preparedness.

For instance, scientists funded by the European Space Agency are working on a digital twin of the Mediterranean Basin to simulate the terrestrial water cycle in the area. This high-resolution virtual replica will help various stakeholders, from citizens to decision-makers, run interactive simulations that will help prevent water-related disasters and manage water resources.

**The role of the metaverse**

**The metaverse** or 3D internet, with its immersive 3D environment, can significantly enhance the capabilities of digital twins in simulating and preparing for disaster scenarios. Here's how:

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/668e215d8bbef917ac3b8892/678025c205748838ebf0c8c0_8xePaF0WQcAYFTcvPW00djd06uo.png)

### Predictive analysis and early warning systems

**Earthquake prediction**

By continuously monitoring real-time data, digital twins can detect subtle changes in seismic activity, such as minor tremors or shifts in tectonic plates, that might indicate the likelihood of an impending earthquake. Advanced algorithms and predictive analytics within the digital twin can analyze these data patterns to forecast potential earthquake events. These predictions include estimating an earthquake's possible magnitude, location, and timing. Such predictive capabilities allow for early warning systems to be activated, giving communities and authorities precious time to implement emergency measures, evacuate vulnerable areas, and mobilize resources.

For instance, Japan utilizes digital twin technology to enhance disaster resilience against frequent earthquakes, typhoons, and heavy rainfall. One Concern, a resilience analytics technology company, uses digital twin technology in Japan to anticipate extreme disasters and climate risks and how they will impact infrastructure like power grids, transportation networks, and local communities. The technology helps businesses in the country measure and reduce potential risks of disasters.

**Flood forecasting**

Digital twins can simulate the region's water bodies, rainfall, and groundwater by integrating real-time data from ground sensors and satellites. Digital replicas of river catchment areas are also crucial in predicting flooding hazards. These models incorporate detailed geospatial and environmental data to represent the entire ecosystem, river geometry, and floodplain characteristics. By simulating river behavior under various conditions, digital twins provide insights into how river systems may respond to changing weather patterns.

Real-time weather condition data, such as precipitation forecasts, temperature variations, and atmospheric conditions, are integrated into the simulations. By combining river data with weather information, digital twins can predict how weather events like heavy rainfall or storms may trigger potential flooding. Using historical data and predictive analytics algorithms, digital twins forecast potential flooding events, helping authorities prepare and respond effectively.

Land managers, urban planners, and ecologists can test various scenarios by adjusting variables like rainfall intensity, river flow rates, and soil saturation levels to simulate different flood scenarios. These tests allow them to assess the impact of various factors on flooding events and identify critical areas at risk. These visualizations help stakeholders understand complex data, trends, and patterns, facilitating decision-making and response planning.

In the Netherlands, climate change makes it crucial to reinforce the region's dikes or embankments, such as the Lekdijk. Sogelink's innovative digital twin technology provides a precise virtual model of the dike, allowing targeted strengthening based on detailed soil knowledge. This approach saves time and costs by focusing on specific areas rather than the entire dike. Sogelink's technology ensures efficient and effective flood protection for the future.

One Concern has also collaborated with Kumamoto City in Japan over the past three years to develop a digital twin that models various flood scenarios, including river overflows and coastal storm surges. This system accurately predicts flood risks by integrating weather forecasts and local data, allowing for proactive measures. In August 2021, it successfully forecasted river levels during heavy rainfall, demonstrating its precision. This technology enables Japan to manage climate risks better and improve community preparedness.

### Urban planning and infrastructure resilience

**Simulation of urban environments**

Resilient urban structures are essential in the face of earthquakes, tsunamis, or other catastrophes. Digital twins of city areas and developments play a crucial role in this. By creating detailed virtual models of urban plans, authorities can simulate various disaster scenarios and assess the impact on buildings and infrastructure. This integrated information allows for precise analysis and proactive measures to reinforce weak points.

Urban planners and engineers can use digital twins to ensure structures are designed and built to withstand disasters. This technology enables the identification of vulnerabilities and the implementation of targeted strengthening efforts, making the entire city more resilient. As a result, when disaster strikes, the city's infrastructure is better prepared, minimizing damage and enhancing the safety and well-being of its residents.

Digital twins also aid in scenario planning before crises occur, making cities better prepared for large-scale emergencies. A notable example is Virtual Singapore, a 3D platform that integrates data about physical infrastructure with demographics, population movement, climate, and other factors. Real-time data helps city administration analyze how different structures and areas respond to earthquakes or floods. Planners can identify potential weaknesses and reinforce them proactively. This ensures that new developments are resilient and capable of withstanding disasters, ultimately safeguarding the city's infrastructure and its residents.

**Infrastructure monitoring**

The digital twin continuously collects data from sensors embedded within physical structures in an urban area, providing detailed information on its performance and condition. This real-time monitoring capability allows engineers and maintenance personnel to quickly and accurately assess the structure's health. By analyzing data from the digital twin, potential structural issues such as fractures, deformation, or vibrations can be identified early, enabling proactive maintenance and interventions. One of the key benefits of using digital twin technology for structural health monitoring is its ability to gather real-time data. Traditionally, structural assessments were often conducted through periodic inspections or manual measurements, which were time-consuming and limited in scope. However, digital twins offer a comprehensive view of the structure's condition by instantly analyzing continuous sensor data streams.

Additionally, integrating various data sources and computational models enhances the digital twin's understanding of structural behavior. This capability enables predictive analysis, forecasting potential faults or performance deterioration based on the collected data. As a result, preventive maintenance measures can be implemented to minimize downtime, optimize resource use, and ensure the structure's and its occupants' safety.

The City of Helsinki has developed a digital twin for infrastructure monitoring as part of the Helsinki 3D+ project, a comprehensive digital model of the city. This city-scale digital twin integrates data from laser scanning, photogrammetry, and other sensors to provide a detailed and accurate representation of all infrastructure, buildings, and public spaces. It enables real-time monitoring and predictive analytics to address potential issues and optimize city systems proactively.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/668e215d8bbef917ac3b8892/678025c02251737727c8298d_DN5slsXtfK4pBFI4Rj5vAAHAQv8.png)

### Environmental monitoring

**Climate change impact assessment**

Digital twins offer a sophisticated climate modeling and simulation platform by replicating Earth's ecosystems, oceans, and atmosphere in a virtual environment. This digital replication allows scientists to simulate various climate scenarios, predict changes, and understand the potential impacts of specific interventions. Such capabilities are invaluable for assessing the effectiveness of climate policies, land-use planning, and deploying renewable energy solutions.

Digital twins also have significant potential for marine spatial planning to manage seas and oceans better and sustainably. The European Digital Twin of the Ocean, announced by President von der Leyen in 2022, aims to provide accessible ocean knowledge through interactive visualization tools. These tools support marine habitat restoration, a sustainable blue economy, and climate adaptation.

In Grenada, a country in the Caribbean, the government uses digital twin technology to create a replica of the country equipped with environmental data to help draft sustainability plans against heat, rainfall, and saltwater intrusion. Similarly, in Spain, authorities are working with Hexagon to create a digital twin of the Campo de Cartagena region, running simulations to predict and prevent environmental issues. This initiative helps mitigate climate change effects by visually comparing ecological changes and potential solutions.

**Hazardous material tracking**

In industrial settings, digital twin technology can monitor and predict equipment maintenance needs, reducing downtime and improving reliability. By monitoring equipment performance in real-time, operators can detect potential issues before they escalate and take corrective action to prevent long wait times and other disruptions.

Digital twins help refine response plans by simulating emergency scenarios and ensuring safety protocols are regularly tested and optimized. Additionally, integrating real-time sensor data enables predictive maintenance and prevents equipment failures and associated safety incidents.

For instance, a virtual replica of a chemical production plant can reduce industrial accidents. Chemical production processes can be dangerous as they involve handling hazardous materials and require proper management. Digital virtual models can monitor, simulate, and optimize real-time operations and production processes to identify potential hazards and risks in multiple scenarios. For instance, a digital twin can identify potential hazards like leaks or spills and alert engineers to take the necessary steps to mitigate the impact.

--- SOURCE 3: Advancing Emergency Care With Digital Twins - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/

SUMMARY:
This transformation in emergency care delivery demonstrates the broader potential of digital twin technology to revolutionize urban health care systems through data-driven, predictive approaches to resource management. By synthesizing real-time patient data with historical health records and predictive algorithms, digital twins facilitate more accurate disease modeling, enhancing clinical decision-making and tailoring interventions to individual patient profiles. During public health crises like COVID-19, digital twins have proven effective in enhancing emergency response capabilities by simulating real-time data collection and vaccination processes to identify optimal resource allocation strategies and refine contingency planning. Digital public health enables optimized resource allocation, reduces waste, and enhances emergency response efficiency, and it allows real-time monitoring of patient anomalies to accelerate emergency interventions.

Abstract
--------

Digital twins—dynamic and real-time simulations of systems or environments—represent a paradigm shift in emergency medicine. We explore their applications across prehospital care, in-hospital management, and recovery. By integrating real-time data, wearable technology, and predictive analytics, digital twins hold the promise of optimizing resource allocation, advancing precision medicine, and tailoring rehabilitation strategies. Moreover, we discuss the challenges associated with their implementation, including data resolution, biological heterogeneity, and ethical considerations, emphasizing the need for actionable frameworks that balance innovation with data governance and public trust.

**Keywords:** emergency care, digital twin, prehospital emergency care, in-hospital emergency care, recovery

Introduction
------------

The concept of digital twins—dynamic, real-time simulations of processes, systems, or environments—has garnered increasing attention across various domains, from urban planning to medical monitoring [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref1)]. Unlike predictive analytics and artificial intelligence (AI)–driven simulations, digital twins construct dynamic mirror models of physical entities through real-time data synchronization and simulation technologies, emphasizing virtual to physical mapping and closed-loop feedback to support full lifecycle management. In contrast, predictive analytics focus on trend forecasting based on historical data, whereas AI-driven simulations concentrate on multiscenario modeling of complex systems. The advantages of digital twins lie in their real-time capabilities, dynamic optimization, and potential for cross-system collaboration, enabling a closed-loop process from monitoring to decision-making, significantly enhancing efficiency and precision [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref2)]. Emergency care, characterized by its demand for timeliness, precision, and adaptability, provides an ideal testing ground for this transformative technology. By bridging prehospital care, in-hospital management, and recovery, digital twins promise to reshape emergency medical systems for the future ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#figure1)). [Textbox 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#box1) provides an explanation of the application of digital twins in prehospital first aid, in-hospital first aid, and rehabilitation.

The role of digital twins in transforming emergency medicine.

### The application of digital twins in prehospital first aid, in-hospital first aid, and rehabilitation.

**Prehospital emergency care**

*   Optimized resource allocation: simulation of real-time first-aid scenarios to assess urban emergency needs and spatial distribution of resources, allowing for evidence-based planning and improved efficiency in resource use

*   Reduced response time: minimization of procedural delays through real-time data sharing, enabling seamless communication and critical data updates across emergency personnel and systems

*   Real-time patient monitoring: leveraging of wearable devices and sensors to create dynamic health profiles, facilitating immediate assessments and predictions of patient conditions (eg, vital signs such as heart rate and blood pressure)

*   Risk stratification: aggregation and analysis of patient data to provide superior accuracy in predicting acute events (eg, cardiac arrests), outperforming traditional clinical risk assessments

*   Enhanced training: use of simulation-based training environments to strengthen emergency response protocols, improve decision-making, and foster team coordination under high-pressure conditions

**In-hospital emergency care**

*   Advanced imaging analysis: integration of multidimensional data to overcome sample limitations in medical imaging, enabling the development of robust diagnostic models

*   Precision medicine: combination of genomic, environmental, and lifestyle data for tailored risk assessments and personalized treatment plans, empowering informed clinical decision-making

*   Human digital models: use of digital replicas of anatomical structures to plan surgeries and minimize risks during procedures by simulating operations beforehand

*   Digital twin–enabled decision-making: facilitation of real-time analysis and expert-guided interventions (eg, cardiopulmonary resuscitation and drug administration) via remote systems, reducing disparities in emergency care accessibility

*   Predictive disease monitoring: use of Internet of Things devices and wearables to continuously monitor patient status, identifying early signs of complications and supporting timely interventions

*   Streamlined resource coordination: ensuring efficient resource distribution and effective communication among health care teams through unified, up-to-date patient data sharing

**Recovery**

*   Continuous monitoring: use of sensors and wearable devices to track recovery progress and dynamically adjust treatment plans to optimize outcomes

*   Telerehabilitation management: enabling remote monitoring of patients through digital twins, providing clinicians with tools to assess and adjust rehabilitation remotely, particularly for mobility-impaired individuals

*   Patient self-management: sharing digital twin insights with patients to encourage active participation in health management through lifestyle modifications and adherence to care plans

*   Disease progression monitoring systems: integration of real-time monitoring data to predict the risk of disease exacerbations or complications, thereby intervening in time and triggering early warnings, allowing physicians to adjust treatment during critical windows and improve outcomes.

Prehospital Emergency Care
--------------------------

Current prehospital emergency care systems often struggle with suboptimal resource allocation due to their reliance on simplified decision-making models that fail to account for the complex, dynamic nature of urban environments [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref3)]. Digital twins address these limitations by unifying diverse data streams into a single, adaptable model that enables real-time optimization of emergency resource allocation. These models incorporate multiple layers of data, from historical emergency call patterns to real-time traffic conditions, enabling more nuanced and effective resource deployment decisions.

Digital twins prove particularly valuable in prehospital emergency care, where time-sensitive decisions directly impact patient outcomes. By incorporating high-resolution information about building layouts, land use, population density, and traffic patterns, digital twins create actionable urban simulations that allow emergency response teams to plan and execute their operations with unprecedented efficiency. The integration of these various data sources enables more sophisticated approaches to emergency resource management than previously possible with traditional systems.

This transformation in emergency care delivery demonstrates the broader potential of digital twin technology to revolutionize urban health care systems through data-driven, predictive approaches to resource management. Moreover, wearables in ambulances provide dynamic health profiles, supporting real-time monitoring and accurate risk stratification for acute events. Simulation-based training environments can help improve emergency team readiness and coordination under high-pressure conditions.

In-Hospital Emergency Care
--------------------------

In hospitals, digital twins are redefining precision medicine by integrating diverse data sources to enable robust diagnostics, personalized treatments, and streamlined workflows [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref4)]. By synthesizing real-time patient data with historical health records and predictive algorithms, digital twins facilitate more accurate disease modeling, enhancing clinical decision-making and tailoring interventions to individual patient profiles. This data-driven approach supports the early detection of complications, optimization of treatment pathways, and improvement of patient outcomes.

Preoperative simulations represent a critical application of digital twins in surgical care. By creating highly accurate digital replicas of patient anatomy, clinicians can simulate surgical procedures, anticipate potential challenges, and refine techniques before making the first incision. These virtual rehearsals significantly reduce surgical risks, improve procedural efficiency, and enhance patient safety. Complementing this, predictive monitoring through Internet of Things (IoT) devices enables the continuous assessment of patient vital signs, offering early warnings of complications such as sepsis or cardiac arrest. These alerts allow medical teams to respond swiftly, averting adverse outcomes and saving lives.

An emerging and transformative application is the development of medical device twins (MDTs), which provide virtual counterparts for critical hospital equipment, including ventilators, defibrillators, infusion pumps, and imaging devices. MDTs are created through digital twin technology and are consistent with the actual medical device in terms of function, performance, and status. They can be updated in real time to reflect the actual operation of the medical device. These digital twins continuously monitor device performance, tracking metrics such as wear and tear, calibration accuracy, and operational efficiency. By identifying potential failures before they occur, MDTs facilitate proactive maintenance, reducing downtime and ensuring the reliability of life-saving equipment during critical emergencies.

Beyond maintenance, MDTs contribute to resource optimization. By analyzing use patterns and forecasting demand surges, these systems can dynamically allocate equipment to areas of highest need, such as during mass casualty incidents or seasonal patient influxes. This predictive capability not only prevents bottlenecks in care delivery but also enhances hospital operational efficiency, ensuring that critical resources are always available when and where they are needed.

Recovery
--------

In recovery, digital twins play a transformative role by enabling personalized and adaptive rehabilitation processes. These advanced systems integrate comprehensive patient data, including clinical records, biometric parameters, and lifestyle information, to develop dynamic treatment plans tailored to individual needs. Wearable devices continuously monitor patient progress in real time, providing crucial data to adjust therapies dynamically, ensuring that interventions remain effective and responsive to changes in a patient’s condition [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref5),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref6)]. This real-time feedback loop facilitates precision rehabilitation, minimizing setbacks and promoting steady recovery trajectories.

Telerehabilitation further amplifies these benefits by offering remote monitoring and therapy-adjustment capabilities. Patients can receive professional guidance and care modification without frequent in-person visits, reducing logistical burdens and enhancing access to rehabilitation services. Moreover, digital twins empower patients by offering insights into their recovery progress, encouraging active engagement in health management and fostering adherence to prescribed care plans.

A significant breakthrough in the recovery phase is disease relapse prediction and early warning, a feature that underscores the predictive power of digital twins. By leveraging longitudinal patient data and advanced predictive analytics, digital twin systems can detect subtle patterns indicative of potential disease recurrence. These insights enable the generation of real-time alerts for health care providers, prompting timely interventions that can mitigate complications and prevent hospital readmissions. For instance, changes in respiratory patterns or biomarkers could signal the early stages of a relapse, allowing for preemptive adjustments in treatment strategies.

Digital Twins in Real-World Applications
----------------------------------------

Currently, the application of digital twins in emergency care remains in its early developmental stages, with significant room for growth, yet they have already demonstrated potential in real-world scenarios. For instance, Pfizer and IBM’s collaborative Project BlueSky focuses on developing remote patient monitoring systems that use wearable devices and environmental sensors to track disease symptoms in real time, capture dynamic data, and automatically identify abnormalities for timely intervention [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref7)]. Similarly, Digital Orthopaedics and Dassault Systèmes jointly created a clinical decision support system capable of generating patient-specific computational models to reduce trial-and-error risks and achieve precision treatment [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref7)]. During public health crises like COVID-19, digital twins have proven effective in enhancing emergency response capabilities by simulating real-time data collection and vaccination processes to identify optimal resource allocation strategies and refine contingency planning. These examples underscore the transformative role of digital twin technology in advancing emergency care efficiency and resilience [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref8)].

In the cases mentioned above, digital twins have demonstrated significant advantages over current public health systems. Digital public health enables optimized resource allocation, reduces waste, and enhances emergency response efficiency, and it allows real-time monitoring of patient anomalies to accelerate emergency interventions. Centered on data-driven and intelligent capabilities, these advancements markedly improve the efficiency of resource allocation, response speed, and decision-making precision in emergency care.

In summary, digital twins represent a reliable tool in the era of digital intelligence, becoming increasingly feasible in the current data-rich context. The emergency response system serves as an excellent testing ground for digital twin applications, offering significant potential across areas such as emergency resource planning, patient monitoring, and rehabilitation. However, the implementation of digital twins in the emergency domain is not a task to be completed overnight. It necessitates advancements in complementary fields, including urban modeling, wearable technology, and the IoT, to fully realize its potential. Despite their promise, the application of digital twins in emergency care faces substantial challenges. Currently, many medical digital twins remain confined to visualization, providing static representations rather than actionable tools for decision-making. For full realization, these systems must evolve beyond visualization to actionable frameworks. A second critical hurdle is data quality and resolution. Biological heterogeneity significantly influences disease progression and treatment outcomes, requiring vast, high-resolution datasets to build robust, accurate models. Lastly, ethical, privacy, and security concerns pose significant barriers to the adoption of digital twins in health care [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12053090/#ref9)]. The integration of sensitive patient data with urban-level modeling raises important questions about data governance, requiring careful consideration to balance innovation with public trust. To address the aforementioned challenges, it is necessary to systematically overcome existing bottlenecks through technological optimization, privacy protection, ethical governance, and multiparty collaboration. On the privacy and security front, full lifecycle data protection must be strengthened by leveraging differential privacy and blockchain technology to anonymize sensitive information and enable traceable storage while implementing dynamic access controls to restrict unnecessary data access. Ethically and legally, clear boundaries and transparent guidelines for technology use must be established. Additionally, collaboration among governments, medical institutions, and technology companies is needed to develop unified technical standards and security evaluation systems alongside public education to build societal trust and ensure patient consent and data authorization. Only through parallel advancements in technology, regulatory frameworks, ethical constraints, and social collaboration can the safe implementation and sustainable development of digital twin technology in emergency scenarios be achieved.

Imagine a future where we can accurately predict and prepare for natural disasters, saving countless lives.

Digital twins in disaster management can make this possible.

Traditional methods often fall short when dealing with climate change and natural disasters, which caused global economic losses of $380 billion in 2023 alone. Earthquakes and floods were the most destructive, causing $99 billion and $94 billion in damages, respectively.

[Digital twin solutions](https://www.toobler.com/digital-twin-solutions) can help by simulating natural disasters and supporting real-time decision-making, reducing the impact.

But exactly[what is a digital twin](https://www.toobler.com/blog/what-is-digital-twin), and how does it improve disaster management? Let’s explore!

Digital Twin in Disaster Management
-----------------------------------

A[](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin)[digital twin](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin) is a virtual, real-time replica of a physical system, product, or procedure. It gathers information from sensors and applies sophisticated analytics to it. Then, it produces a dynamic[](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-model)[digital twin model](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-model)that replicates the actions of its real-world equivalent.

Parallelly, digital twins in disaster management refer to the development of virtual representations of infrastructure, towns, or landscapes to anticipate and react to natural disasters such as floods, wildfires, and earthquakes.

Expert digital twin solution providers are essential when incorporating cutting-edge technology like artificial intelligence and the IoT into disaster preparedness. By allowing governments and organizations to model natural disaster scenarios, these providers assist them in:

*   Accurately forecast the effects of a calamity.

*   Create efficient plans for evacuation.

*   Managing resource allocation for relief activities.

For example, a digital twin of an earthquake-prone city can assess building weaknesses and provide mitigation strategies before an earthquake. This degree of readiness goes beyond conventional approaches, which frequently depend on past data and static models.

**Also read:**[How Digital Twins and IoT Work Together [With Example]](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-iot)

What Makes Digital Twins Better Than Conventional Approaches?
-------------------------------------------------------------

Reactive tactics, antiquated simulations, and static data have long been the mainstays of traditional disaster management techniques.

Digital twin solution providers transform this by providing:

*   **Real-Time Data Integration**: Conventional approaches cannot quickly adjust to changing circumstances. Digital twins automatically update scenarios based on real-time sensor data.

*   **Precise Forecasts:**By utilizing artificial intelligence and machine learning, digital twins examine complex variables and provide significantly more dependable forecasts than previous trend research.

*   **Cost-effectiveness:**Using digital twins to invest in safety precautions can save billions of dollars in possible losses and human lives.

*   **Interactive Simulations:** Digital twins, instead of static models, let stakeholders evaluate several crisis scenarios, maximizing results while preparing for the worst-case scenario.

Suggested read: What are the benefits of digital twin?

Use Cases of Digital Twin Technology in Disaster Management
-----------------------------------------------------------

### ![Image 10](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/2048x1280/05c49cba26/use-cases-of-digital-twin-technology-in-disaster-management.webp)**1. Simulating Earthquake Impacts to Improve Building Resilience**

Did you know that earthquakes cost the world's economy more than $75 billion each year? Cities can use digital twin technology to model how seismic waves affect infrastructure and buildings. Engineers can use these models to pinpoint structural weaknesses and improve designs for resilience.

Digital twins, for example, have been used to simulate earthquake scenarios in Japan, allowing authorities to lower risks and strengthen vital infrastructure.

Urban planners can prioritize safety measures and identify the most susceptible sections of a city by building a virtual duplicate of it. This proactive strategy surpasses traditional approaches, which frequently rely on antiquated risk assessments.

### **2. Flood Management Through Predictive Analytics and Digital Twins**

One of the most common natural disasters in the world, flooding, causes economic disruption and relocation. Digital twins excel in this situation. They can anticipate floods before they occur by combining real-time data from IoT sensors in drainage systems, reservoirs, and rivers.

Digital twin models, for instance, are used by Dutch authorities to anticipate rising flood levels and schedule evacuations in advance. By analyzing factors like rainfall, soil absorption rates, and urban drainage capacity, these models help cities prevent damage and mitigate it.

Imagine that cities could use natural disaster simulations to improve water flow and strengthen barriers, preventing floods rather than responding to them. This would completely change the dynamics of the game.

**Further read:**[10 Examples of Digital Twin Technologies for Industries](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-examples)

### **3. Forest Fire Prevention and Mitigation Using Real-Time Simulations**

What would be the scenario if you could forecast the location of the next wildfire?

The amount of land destroyed by forest fires rose by roughly 5.4% a year in 2023. Forest fires lose nearly 6 million extra hectares of tree cover annually.

[Digital twins and AI](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-and-ai) are increasingly utilized to predict possible fire outbreaks. This also helps to track environmental factors, including temperature, wind speed, and humidity.

For instance, Australia has implemented digital twins to simulate the development of forest fires, allowing firefighters to plan containment measures efficiently. Additionally, these models guarantee faster reaction times by directing resources to high-risk areas.

By simulating fire spread patterns with digital twins, authorities can create effective evacuation plans and prevent flames from getting worse.

### ![Image 11](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/637x473/f4443d79ec/world-resource-institute.png)**4. Forecasting Tsunamis and Protecting the Coast**

Tsunamis are among the deadliest natural disasters because they are caused by underwater earthquakes and provide little warning. Digital twins of coastal areas include oceanic information, such as seismic activity and tidal patterns, to:

*   Predict possible tsunami situations and their effects.

*   Make the most of buffer zones and seawalls along the coast.

*   Create and implement quick evacuation plans.

Digital twins are used to mimic tsunami scenarios and help coastal towns in Japan prepare for unforeseen disasters.

### **5. Monitoring and Preventing Landslides**

Landslides are a severe hazard in mountainous areas and are frequently brought on by intense rains or seismic activity. Digital twins track geotechnical data in real-time, such as rainfall patterns, slope stability, and soil composition. In doing so, they:

*   Estimate the chance of landslides in susceptible locations.

*   Make it possible for high-risk areas to have reinforced infrastructure.

*   Notify communities in danger in real-time.

Examples of Natural Disaster Simulations Using AI and Digital Twins
-------------------------------------------------------------------

### **1. Forecasting Hurricane Paths**

Imagine the seas bursting into a hurricane. Authorities can estimate the storm's possible course, intensity, and impact by combining digital twins of coastal areas with AI-powered models.

[According to a study](https://oneconcern.com/en/news/hurricane-ian-2022-putting-our-digital-twin-to-the-test/), digital twins and AI-driven systems were used in the United States to forecast Hurricane Ian’s path in 2022. This aided in resource allocation and evacuation.

### **2. Simulation of Wildfire Spread**

AI is changing the usually unpredictable nature of wildfires. Wildfire spread patterns are simulated using digital twins of woods and environmental data such as wind speed and dryness levels.

[According to research](https://www.preventionweb.net/news/fighting-fires-space-record-time-how-ai-could-prevent-repeat-australias-devastating-wildfires), Australia uses artificial intelligence (AI) to monitor fire-prone areas and mimic containment tactics to save numerous ecosystems and communities.

### **3. Infrastructure Stress Testing**

AI prepares infrastructure for calamities in addition to predicting them.[](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-for-buildings-benefits)[Digital twins for buildings](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-for-buildings-benefits), bridges, and dams replicate stress situations to ensure they can survive severe weather.

In[](https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/shows/2090042/)[tsunami-prone regions of Japan](https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/shows/2090042/), AI-enhanced digital twins have been crucial in the design of earthquake-resistant structures.

**Also read:**[AI in Digital Twin: Revolutionizing Predictive Maintenance and Operational Efficiency](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-ai-predictive-maintenance-efficiency)

Why Are Digital Twins and AI a Perfect Fit?
-------------------------------------------

AI gives digital twins’ visualizations more intelligence. AI provides predictive skills that ensure proactive disaster management, while digital twins provide a virtual replica. When combined, they offer:

*   Speed: Real-time updates and analysis for changing crises.

*   Accurate: Predictive models with small error margins.

*   Scalability: The capacity to concurrently monitor and model large geographic areas.

The combination is a life-saving tool that goes beyond technology.

Challenges and Future Scope
---------------------------

### **Challenges of Digital Twin in Disaster Management**

Digital twins have the potential to revolutionize our approach to disaster preparedness and response, but there are challenges along the way.

Let's examine the opportunities and challenges that digital twins in disaster management may face.

**Also read:**[Overcoming Digital Twin Implementation Hurdles | Toobler](https://www.toobler.com/blog/challenges-in-digital-twin-implementation)

#### **1. Data Security and Privacy**

Real-time data collection from sensors, cameras, and other devices is crucial to digital twins. However, this data presents serious privacy issues, even if it is crucial for producing realistic natural disaster models.

For instance, IoT devices in smart cities gather information on everything from water levels to traffic patterns. If this data is not encrypted or stored safely, it is susceptible to cyberattacks.

Solution: These risks can be reduced by implementing GDPR-compliant procedures, strong cybersecurity safeguards, and end-to-end encryption.

**Please read:**[Digital Twin Security: Steps to Take & Best Practices](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-security)

#### **2. Complexity of Integration**

Several systems are involved in disaster management, including emergency response coordination, infrastructure monitoring, weather forecasting, and geographic mapping. Combining these disparate technologies into a coherent digital twin model is challenging.

*   Many businesses need help with compatibility problems when integrating contemporary digital twin platforms with legacy systems.

*   The requirement for cross-disciplinary cooperation between local authorities and digital twin solution providers frequently slows implementation.

**Also read:**[Importance of Real-Time Integration in Digital Twin](https://www.toobler.com/blog/real-time-integration-in-digital-twins)

#### **3. High Initial Expenses**

To build and implement a digital twin, advanced hardware, AI capabilities, and qualified experts are needed. The initial outlay of funds might be a major obstacle for small cities or undeveloped countries.

Nonetheless, the savings from lesser disaster recovery expenses frequently justify the expenditure in the long run.

**Further read:**[How much does it cost to develop a digital twin](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-cost-development)

### **Future Scope: What’s Next for Digital Twins in Disaster Management?**

#### **1. Increased Use of AI-Powered Twins**

Digital twins will get smarter as AI technology develops. Machine learning algorithms that improve simulations will allow cities to prepare for currently unpredictably occurring disasters, such as urban heat waves or compounded disasters (e.g., earthquakes producing tsunamis).

#### **2. Global Collaboration**

Imagine a scenario in which nations coordinate international relief efforts by sharing real-time disaster simulations. This vision is coming true as organizations like the UN incorporate digital twin technology into frameworks for responding to global disasters.

#### **3. Affordable Options for Developing Countries**

Innovation frequently spreads. Increased research and development should make cost-effective digital twin systems designed for smaller economies available, expanding access to them.
